nodes.name: Wars & Conflicts
nodes.description: Major wars and conflicts throughout world history

items.prompt: Which war (431–404 BCE) pitted Athens against Sparta and ended with the defeat of the Athenian Empire, described in detail by historian Thucydides?
items.explanation:
Athens (Delian League) vs. Sparta (Peloponnesian League), 431–404 BCE
Thucydides wrote *History of the Peloponnesian War* — the definitive ancient account, a model of historical methodology
A plague struck Athens in 430 BCE, killing ~25% of the population including the statesman Pericles
Sparta won with Persian financial support; Athens was forced to demolish its Long Walls and disband its fleet
Victory weakened all Greek city-states, enabling Macedonian domination under Philip II and Alexander the Great
Thucydides identified Spartan fear of growing Athenian power as the true cause — a dynamic later scholars call the 'Thucydides Trap'

items.prompt: What series of three wars between Rome and Carthage, fought from 264 to 146 BCE, featured Hannibal crossing the Alps with war elephants and ended with Rome razing Carthage to the ground?
items.explanation:
Three wars between Rome and Carthage (264–146 BCE); 'Punic' from Latin 'Poenicus' (Phoenician)
First Punic War (264–241 BCE): Rome built its first navy to contest Sicilian territory and won
Second Punic War (218–201 BCE): Hannibal crossed the Alps with war elephants; crushed Rome at Cannae (216 BCE), killing ~50,000 Romans in one day
Scipio Africanus defeated Hannibal at Zama (202 BCE), ending Carthaginian power
Third Punic War (149–146 BCE): Rome besieged and razed Carthage completely; 50,000 survivors sold into slavery
Senator Cato the Elder ended every Senate speech with 'Carthago delenda est' ('Carthage must be destroyed')

items.prompt: Which October 1066 battle saw William the Conqueror defeat King Harold II of England, beginning the Norman conquest?
items.explanation:
Fought October 14, 1066 near Hastings, East Sussex; William, Duke of Normandy vs. King Harold II
Harold had just defeated another invasion (Harald Hardrada of Norway) at Stamford Bridge three weeks earlier
Harold reportedly killed by an arrow through the eye — depicted in the Bayeux Tapestry (70-meter embroidered narrative)
Norman conquest transformed English: French became the language of court, giving English roughly half its vocabulary
William commissioned the Domesday Book (1086) — a comprehensive census of England's land and resources
One of the most consequential battles in English history; Norman feudalism permanently altered English law, society, and architecture

items.prompt: What name is given to the series of religious wars (1095–1291) launched by European Christians to capture the Holy Land from Muslim powers?
items.explanation:
Nine major Crusades from 1095 (Pope Urban II's call at Clermont) to 1271–1291
First Crusade (1096–1099) succeeded: Crusaders captured Jerusalem in 1099 and massacred its Muslim and Jewish population
Saladin (Salah ad-Din) retook Jerusalem in 1187, triggering the Third Crusade led by Richard I 'Lionheart' of England
Fourth Crusade (1202–1204): diverted by Venice and sacked Constantinople — fellow Christian city — in 1204
Failed strategically: all Crusader states were eventually lost; last stronghold Acre fell in 1291
Lasting legacy: opened Silk Road trade routes to Europe, transmitted classical knowledge, and created deep Christian-Muslim antagonism

items.prompt: Which conflict (1337–1453) was fought between England and France over the French throne, famously turned by a teenage French peasant girl who claimed to hear divine voices?
items.explanation:
England vs. France, 1337–1453 (actually 116 years, fought intermittently)
Trigger: English king Edward III claimed the French throne via his French mother, Isabella
Joan of Arc (1412–1431) led French forces to key victories before being captured and burned at the stake as a heretic by the English-allied Burgundians
Ended with France expelling England from all continental territory except Calais
English longbow dominated early battles: Crécy (1346) and Agincourt (1415), where Henry V defeated a far larger French army
Aftermath in England: the dynastic instability sparked the Wars of the Roses (1455–1487) between Lancaster and York
items.cardFront: England vs. France, Joan of Arc, 1337–1453

items.prompt: Which devastating Central European conflict (1618–1648), begun by the Defenestration of Prague, transformed from a religious war into a broader power struggle and ended with the Peace of Westphalia?
items.explanation:
1618–1648 in the Holy Roman Empire; began as Catholic vs. Protestant conflict before drawing in all major European powers
Triggered by the Defenestration of Prague (1618): Protestant Bohemian nobles threw Catholic Habsburg officials from a castle window
France (Catholic) backed the Protestant side to weaken the Habsburgs — demonstrating the war's shift from religion to power politics
Peace of Westphalia (1648) established the principle of state sovereignty and religious tolerance — foundation of the modern nation-state system
Killed ~8 million people, mainly through famine and disease; Central European population declined 25–40%
Sweden emerged as a major power; France under Richelieu weakened the Habsburgs and became Europe's dominant land power
items.cardFront: 1618–1648, Defenestration of Prague, Peace of Westphalia

items.prompt: Which 1756–1763 conflict — fought across five continents by all major European powers — established Britain as the dominant global empire and is sometimes called the first world war?
items.explanation:
Fought 1756–1763 across Europe, North America, the Caribbean, West Africa, India, and the Philippines
Known as the French and Indian War in North America, where Britain defeated France for colonial supremacy
Britain emerged as the dominant global power, gaining Canada, Florida, and India under the Treaty of Paris (1763)
Prussian King Frederick the Great's genius allowed Prussia to survive simultaneous war against France, Austria, Russia, and Sweden
Considered a true world war: five continents, seven years, every major European power involved
Britain's war debt led to new taxes on American colonists — the Stamp Act (1765) and others — directly causing the American Revolution

items.prompt: Which series of conflicts (1803–1815) saw Napoleon Bonaparte's France dominate most of Europe before his final defeat at Waterloo?
items.explanation:
Series of wars 1803–1815 between Napoleonic France and shifting coalitions of European powers (up to six coalitions)
At peak, France controlled territory from Spain to the borders of Russia, and Napoleon had installed family members as kings
Disastrous 1812 Russian campaign: of 600,000 soldiers who entered Russia, fewer than 100,000 returned — Russian winter and scorched-earth tactics proved decisive
Napoleon defeated at Leipzig (1813, 'Battle of the Nations') and exiled to Elba; escaped and returned for the Hundred Days
Final defeat at Waterloo (June 18, 1815) by Wellington (Britain) and Blücher (Prussia)
Congress of Vienna (1815) redrew Europe's map and created the balance-of-power system that lasted until WWI

items.prompt: Which June 1815 battle — Napoleon's final defeat at the hands of Wellington and Blücher — ended the Hundred Days and led to Napoleon's exile to Saint Helena?
items.explanation:
Fought June 18, 1815 near Waterloo (modern Belgium); Napoleon vs. Duke of Wellington (Britain) and Field Marshal Blücher (Prussia)
Napoleon had escaped from Elba in March 1815 and retook power in France for just 100 days before this decisive defeat
Napoleon delayed his morning attack until 11 am to let the ground dry; Wellington called victory 'the nearest run thing you ever saw'
Prussian reinforcements arrived in the afternoon and shattered Napoleon's retreating Imperial Guard
Napoleon exiled to Saint Helena (remote South Atlantic island) where he died in 1821
'Meeting one's Waterloo' entered the language as an idiom for a final, crushing defeat

items.prompt: Which two wars (1839–1842 and 1856–1860), fought between Britain (and later France) and Qing China over trade rights, resulted in the cession of Hong Kong and inaugurated China's 'Century of Humiliation'?
items.explanation:
First Opium War (1839–1842): Britain forced China to open trade after China confiscated and destroyed British opium imports
Treaty of Nanking (1842): China ceded Hong Kong Island, opened 5 treaty ports (Shanghai, Canton, etc.) — first of China's 'unequal treaties'
Second Opium War (1856–1860): Britain and France attacked China over an incident involving a Chinese-registered British ship
Anglo-French forces burned and looted the Imperial Summer Palace (Yuanmingyuan) in Beijing (1860)
Treaties of Tientsin legalized the opium trade and opened more ports
Beginning of China's 'Century of Humiliation' (1839–1949): an era of foreign subjugation that shaped modern Chinese national identity
items.cardFront: 1839–1842, 1856–1860 | Britain vs. Qing China | Hong Kong | 'Century of Humiliation'

items.prompt: Which 1853–1856 war — fought mainly in the Crimean Peninsula by Russia against an Ottoman-British-French alliance — became famous for Florence Nightingale's nursing reforms and the disastrous Charge of the Light Brigade?
items.explanation:
Fought 1853–1856; Russia vs. Ottoman Empire, France, Britain, and Sardinia over Russian ambitions in Ottoman territory
Siege of Sevastopol (1854–1855): the 11-month siege was the war's central campaign and Russia's most humiliating failure
Florence Nightingale organized nursing at Scutari hospital, reducing British mortality from ~40% to ~2% — founding modern nursing
Charge of the Light Brigade (October 25, 1854): 600 British cavalry charged Russian artillery due to miscommunication; immortalized by Tennyson's poem
First war extensively covered by telegraph dispatches and journalists — public opinion became a military factor for the first time
Russia's defeat halted its southward expansion and exposed military modernization gaps relative to Western powers

items.prompt: Which 1870–1871 war, in which Prussia decisively defeated France, led to German unification and the humiliating proclamation of the German Empire in the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles?
items.explanation:
Fought 1870–1871; initiated by Bismarck's manipulation of the Ems Dispatch to provoke France into declaring war
Prussian forces besieged Paris and captured Emperor Napoleon III at Sedan (September 1870) — the decisive blow
German Empire proclaimed in the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles on January 18, 1871 — deliberately humiliating France
France ceded Alsace-Lorraine to Germany; France's obsession with recovering it was a direct cause of WWI
Paris Commune (1871): France's short-lived socialist government that lasted 72 days before violent suppression by French troops
Bismarck's masterwork of Realpolitik: isolated France diplomatically, provoked it to attack first, and unified Germany in one campaign

items.prompt: Which 1899–1902 war between the British Empire and two Boer republics in South Africa pioneered the use of concentration camps and ended with British dominance over the region?
items.explanation:
Fought 1899–1902 between Britain and the Transvaal (South African Republic) and Orange Free State (Boer republics)
Boers were descendants of Dutch settlers; Britain wanted control of the Witwatersrand gold fields discovered in 1886
Boers used highly effective guerrilla warfare; Britain responded with scorched-earth tactics and concentration camps
~28,000 Boer civilians (mostly children) and ~20,000 Black Africans died in British concentration camps from disease
Peace of Vereeniging (1902): Boer republics absorbed into the British Empire
Brought severe international criticism of British imperialism; future South African PMs Louis Botha and Jan Smuts were former Boer commanders
items.cardFront: Britain vs. Boer republics, 1899–1902, South Africa

items.prompt: Which 1904–1905 war shocked the world when Japan decisively defeated Russia — the first time a modern Asian nation had defeated a major European power?
items.explanation:
Fought 1904–1905 over control of Manchuria and Korea; Japan launched a surprise attack on the Russian fleet at Port Arthur (February 8, 1904) before declaring war
Battle of Tsushima (May 1905): Japan's navy annihilated Russia's Baltic Fleet after its 18,000-mile voyage from the Baltic Sea
First decisive modern defeat of a European power by an Asian nation — shocked Western assumptions of racial and military superiority
Russia's humiliation triggered the 1905 Russian Revolution (Bloody Sunday, January 22, 1905)
Settled by the Treaty of Portsmouth (1905), mediated by President Theodore Roosevelt, who won the Nobel Peace Prize for it
Inspired anti-colonial independence movements across Asia and Africa
items.cardFront: Japan vs. Russia, 1904–1905, first Asian defeat of European power

items.prompt: Which global war (1914–1918), triggered by Archduke Franz Ferdinand's assassination, resulted in ~20 million deaths and the collapse of four empires?
items.explanation:
Fought 1914–1918 between the Allies (Britain, France, Russia, later USA) and Central Powers (Germany, Austria-Hungary, Ottoman Empire)
Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary assassinated in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914, by Gavrilo Princip of the Black Hand
Trench warfare on the Western Front: Battle of the Somme (1916) saw 60,000 British casualties on Day 1 — the bloodiest in British military history
US entered April 1917 after Germany's unrestricted submarine warfare and the Zimmermann Telegram (Germany proposed Mexico attack the US)
Armistice at the 11th hour of the 11th day of the 11th month (November 11, 1918); collapsed the German, Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman, and Russian Empires
Treaty of Versailles (1919): Article 231 ('war guilt clause') imposed harsh reparations on Germany — widely blamed for creating conditions for WWII

items.prompt: Which 1936–1939 conflict — in which Francisco Franco's Nationalists defeated the Spanish Republic with support from Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy — served as a military testing ground for World War II?
items.explanation:
Fought July 1936–April 1939 between Franco's Nationalists (backed by Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy) and the Republic (backed by the Soviet Union)
Over 500,000 died; ~500,000 fled into exile after Franco's victory
International Brigades: ~32,000 foreign volunteers (including writers like George Orwell and Ernest Hemingway) fought for the Republic
Guernica (April 26, 1937): Nazi Luftwaffe bombed this Basque market town to test aerial bombing tactics; Picasso painted *Guernica* in response
Franco ruled Spain as dictator until his death in 1975, one of the longest surviving fascist regimes
Considered a 'dress rehearsal' for WWII: Germany and Italy tested weapons, tactics, and coordination that they would use in the coming world war

items.prompt: Which global war (1939–1945), begun with Germany's invasion of Poland, caused the most deaths in human history — an estimated 70–85 million — and ended with atomic bombs dropped on Japan?
items.explanation:
Fought 1939–1945 between the Allies (UK, USA, USSR, France) and Axis (Germany, Italy, Japan)
Germany invaded Poland on September 1, 1939; France and Britain declared war two days later
Holocaust: Nazi Germany systematically murdered ~6 million Jews and ~6 million others in concentration and death camps
D-Day (June 6, 1944): Allied invasion of Normandy — 156,000 troops landed on the first day in the largest amphibious invasion in history
Hiroshima (August 6) and Nagasaki (August 9, 1945): US dropped atomic bombs; Japan surrendered August 15, 1945
Deadliest conflict in history: ~70–85 million deaths total, including an estimated 50–55 million civilians

items.prompt: Which 1950–1953 war — often called 'The Forgotten War' — ended in an armistice (not a peace treaty) that left Korea divided near the 38th parallel?
items.explanation:
Fought June 1950–July 1953; North Korea (backed by China and USSR) invaded South Korea on June 25, 1950
UN forces under Gen. Douglas MacArthur pushed North Korea to the Chinese border; China entered with ~300,000 troops in October 1950, driving UN forces south
MacArthur fired by Truman in April 1951 for publicly advocating use of nuclear weapons against China — a constitutional crisis over civilian control of the military
Armistice signed July 27, 1953 — technically no peace treaty exists; North and South Korea remain in a legal state of war
Created the world's most heavily fortified border: the Demilitarized Zone (DMZ) at roughly the 38th parallel
US casualties: 36,574 dead; South Korean casualties: ~137,000 military; estimated 2–3 million total deaths including civilians

items.prompt: Which conflict (1955–1975) ended with North Vietnamese forces capturing Saigon, marking the first military defeat for the United States?
items.explanation:
Fought 1955–1975 between communist North Vietnam (backed by USSR and China) and South Vietnam (backed by USA)
US involvement escalated after the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution (1964) — authorization based on a disputed or fabricated naval incident
Peak US deployment: 543,000 troops in 1969; 58,220 Americans died in total
Tet Offensive (January 30, 1968): coordinated North Vietnamese attacks on 100+ cities shocked Americans and turned public opinion against the war
US withdrew under 'Vietnamization'; Paris Peace Accords signed January 27, 1973
Saigon fell April 30, 1975; Vietnam reunified under communist rule as the Socialist Republic of Vietnam in 1976
items.cardFront: 1955–1975, Saigon fell, first US military defeat

items.prompt: Which June 1967 Arab-Israeli conflict — lasting only six days — resulted in Israel capturing the Sinai Peninsula, Gaza Strip, West Bank, and Golan Heights?
items.explanation:
Fought June 5–10, 1967 between Israel and Egypt, Jordan, and Syria
Israel launched a preemptive air strike on June 5, destroying most of Egypt's air force on the ground within hours
Israel captured Sinai Peninsula and Gaza Strip from Egypt, West Bank from Jordan, and Golan Heights from Syria
Israel tripled its territory in six days and reunified Jerusalem under Israeli control, including the Temple Mount and Western Wall
Created ~300,000 Palestinian refugees from the West Bank and Gaza — the origins of the modern Palestinian refugee crisis
Transformed Middle Eastern geopolitics permanently; territories captured remain central to the Israeli-Palestinian conflict today

items.prompt: Which 1973 war, in which Egypt and Syria launched a surprise attack on Israel on the holiest day of the Jewish calendar, triggered the OPEC oil embargo and a global energy crisis?
items.explanation:
October 6–25, 1973; also called the October War or Ramadan War
Egypt (Anwar Sadat) and Syria coordinated a surprise attack on Israel on Yom Kippur, the holiest day of the Jewish year
Egyptian forces crossed the Suez Canal; Israel was initially caught off guard, then counterattacked and crossed the Canal itself
USA airlifted supplies to Israel; USSR resupplied Egypt and Syria — nuclear alert raised on both sides
OPEC imposed an oil embargo on the US and Western allies → 1973 oil crisis (gas prices quadrupled within months)
Led to Camp David Accords (1978) and Egypt-Israel Peace Treaty (1979); Sadat and Begin shared Nobel Peace Prize (1978)
items.cardFront: October 1973 | Egypt + Syria vs. Israel | surprise Yom Kippur attack | OPEC embargo

items.prompt: Which 1982 conflict between Britain and Argentina, fought over islands in the South Atlantic, ended with British victory and contributed to Argentina's return to democracy?
items.explanation:
Fought April–June 1982; Argentina invaded the British-controlled Falkland Islands (Islas Malvinas) on April 2, 1982
Argentina's military junta under Leopoldo Galtieri ordered the invasion to distract from domestic economic crisis and political unrest
Britain's Task Force sailed 8,000 miles to retake the islands; 74-day conflict ending with Argentine surrender June 14, 1982
Casualties: 255 British and 649 Argentine military deaths; 3 Falkland Islands civilians killed
The junta's defeat discredited Argentina's military government and accelerated the country's return to democracy in 1983
Margaret Thatcher's poll numbers surged after victory — the 'Falklands factor' contributed heavily to her 1983 landslide election win

items.prompt: Which war (1980–1988), the longest conventional war of the 20th century, saw Iraq invade Iran under Saddam Hussein, who deployed chemical weapons including against Kurdish civilians at Halabja?
items.explanation:
September 22, 1980 – August 20, 1988; Iraq invaded Iran citing border disputes and fear of Iran's Islamic Revolution spreading
Iraq (Saddam Hussein) used chemical weapons — mustard gas and nerve agents — against Iranian troops and Kurdish civilians
Halabja massacre (March 16–17, 1988): Iraq killed an estimated 3,200–5,000 Kurdish civilians with chemical weapons
∼500,000 military deaths on each side; ended in stalemate with borders essentially unchanged
The US supported Iraq diplomatically during the war; Iran was still isolated after the 1979-1981 hostage crisis
UN-brokered ceasefire (Resolution 598) on August 20, 1988; the longest conventional war of the 20th century
items.cardFront: 1980–1988 | Iraq vs. Iran | Halabja chemical attack | Saddam Hussein | stalemate

items.prompt: Which 1990–1991 war saw a US-led coalition of 35 nations expel Iraqi forces from Kuwait in Operation Desert Storm?
items.explanation:
Fought August 1990–February 1991 after Iraq invaded Kuwait on August 2, 1990
Saddam Hussein annexed Kuwait, claiming it was historically Iraqi and targeting its oil wealth; the UN condemned the invasion
US-led coalition of 35 nations authorized by UN Security Council; Operation Desert Shield (defense) became Operation Desert Storm (offensive)
Air campaign began January 17, 1991; ground war lasted only 100 hours (February 24–28, 1991)
Coalition drove Iraqi forces from Kuwait with minimal coalition losses (~300 deaths vs. ~25,000+ Iraqi military casualties)
Bush administration stopped short of toppling Saddam Hussein, a decision controversial in retrospect; Saddam remained in power until the 2003 Iraq War
items.cardFront: Iraq vs. Kuwait, 1990–1991, Operation Desert Storm

items.prompt: Which 1992–1995 war in the former Yugoslavia included the Srebrenica massacre — the worst atrocity in Europe since World War II — and ended with the U.S.-brokered Dayton Agreement?
items.explanation:
Bosnia and Herzegovina vs. Republika Srpska (Bosnian Serbs, backed by Serbia), 1992–1995
Followed Bosnia's declaration of independence from Yugoslavia; Bosnian Serbs sought to create ethnically homogeneous territory
Siege of Sarajevo (1992–1996): 1,425 days — the longest siege of a capital city in modern warfare
Srebrenica massacre (July 1995): Bosnian Serb forces under Ratko Mladić killed ~8,000 Bosniak men and boys — ruled genocide by international courts
NATO air strikes broke the siege; Dayton Agreement (November 1995) ended the war
~100,000 people killed; 2.2 million displaced; first European genocide since the Holocaust
